title: 福爾摩沙民族學評註
alternative: Notes on the ethnology of Formosa
description: 史溫侯指出福爾摩沙及周邊附屬地區住著兩個種族──明顯屬於馬來人種的野蠻原住民（aborigines）及其後裔，包含平埔族（Pepos）、傀儡番（Kalees）、生番（Chin hwan）；以及屬於蒙古人種、精於商業（commercially-civilized）的中國墾民。本文敘述1862年中考察福爾摩沙南部地區的原住民。

Read before the British Association [for the Advancement of Science], August 1863.  Printed separately.  London: Frederic Bell, 1863.  With two coloured plates.  Reprinted in Wm. Campbell, Formosa under the Dutch: Described from contemporary records (London: Kegan Paul, 1903), pp. 551-557.  羅伯特・史溫侯。〈福爾摩沙民族學評註〉。摘錄自在民族學學會發表的一篇論文，並附有補充意見。1863年8月於英國科學促進會發表。單獨印刷。倫敦弗雷德里克・貝爾1863年出版。有2幅彩色插圖。威廉・坎貝爾以〈荷蘭統治的福爾摩沙：當代紀錄的描述〉為題重印，倫敦基根・保羅1903年出版，第551-557頁。

##--SPLIT--##
Swinhoe, Robert.  Notes on the ethnology of Formosa.  Extracted from a paper read before the Ethnological Society, with additional remarks. Read before the British Association [for the Advancement of Science], August, 1863.  London: Frederic Bell, 1863.

Notes on the Ethnology of Formosa
[P. 3] The island of Formosa and its dependencies are peopled, broadly speaking, by two races of men – the wild aborigines and their descendants, of apparently the Malay type, and the commercially-civilized Chinese colonists of the Mongol type.  It is my intention in this paper to take a cursory glance at the peoples of the different islands round and about Formosa, before I proceed to consider the inhabitants of the island itself.
To begin with the Pescadores.  I have visited these islands three times, and did not succeed in finding any traces of their original inhabitants.  The indications of their possession by the Dutch are confined to one ruin of what was once a strong square fort.  It stands on the left side as you enter the chief harbour, at the head of which is located the town of Makung, governed by a Heëtai, or naval commandant, responsible to the chief military authority at Taiwanfoo.  This functionary, with a small fleet of dilapidated junks under his command, exercises jurisdiction over the entire group of islands, which are inhabited by Chinese fishermen and farmers – emigrants from South Fokien (or Amoy, Chinchew, and Swatow,) and all speaking dialects of the language generally known as the Teek-chew or Hok-keën.  Lammay island, off the S.W. coast, is peopled by fishermen from Swatow.  The group of islands between the south cape of Formosa and the Philippines, and known as the Bashees, have, I am told, a Malay population similar to the Tagals at Luzon.  Off the S.E. side of Formosa is a small island called Botel Tobago, containing a wild race of Malays, of whom also little is known further than that they are few in number, have no boats or canoes, and depend on the produce of the soil, which they rudely cultivate, as also on fish, for subsistence; and that they live in log huts and dress very scantily.  Further north off the coast stands the island of Sama-sana, colonised again by quite a different race, allied to the Loochooans, but more immediately to the natives of the nearer Madjicosima group.  These people bind up their hair on the top of their heads like the Loochooans, and wear only a blanket-like coat over their naked bodies.  Some years ago a large Hamburg vessel, the "Aar," foundered close off this island, and her crew, consisting of some fourteen men, went to it in a boat.  They had much difficulty in finding a place to land, as the surf breaks heavily all round.  The natives, about 100 in number, at first deserted the village (the only one on the island), with the timidity common to the Loochooan race.  These islanders had also no boats or vessels of any kind.  They cultivate patches of sweet potatoes and rice, and have a few pigs.  The shipwrecked crew conciliated the natives, and were hospitably treated by them for eight days, when a vessel bound to Ningpo relieved them from their distressing situation.  For this, the only information I could procure regarding this and the previously-mentioned island, I am indebted to Captain Meineke, of the Prussian brig "Typhoon," who had made several voyages on the Formosan coast, and had acquired a vast amount of valuable experience.  The smaller islands close adjoining the Formosan shore are all Chinese possessions.
[P. 4] The large straggling city of Taiwanfoo, with its parks and gardens and high walls, situated about five miles from the shore-line, at the edge of a large slightly undulating plain, is entirely Chinese.  In various parts of the city several graves are pointed out by the natives as containing the remains of Hollanders and of black men, but no tablet of any kind was found to ##--SPLIT--## tell of the fact.  The population of this city amounts to perhaps 120,000 souls, from various parts of China, but chiefly from Fokien.  I had heard from the British merchant captains at Ape's Hill that beyond Taiwanfoo lived a race who used Roman characters, and boasted of their origin from the Dutch.  I was naturally anxious to see what kind of hybridism such beings would present, when one morning a military officer, a thoroughly Chinese-looking individual, came to visit me, and informed me that his ancestor was a red-haired man (Dutchman), and was one of 3000 soldiers left in the island during Koksinga's [sic] time, who had shaved their heads and acknowledged allegiance to the Chinese; that his village, Sinkang, chiefly composed of the descendents of these soldiers, was situate [sic] ten miles out of the north gate; and that they still preserved clothes and papers which belonged to their forefathers; that there was a large village further northwards, and one to the southward, composed of their people; and that others of their race, intermarried with Chinese, were distributed over various parts of the island.  I subsequently received a visit from a large number of these people, and was disgusted to find that only a few of the very old men could speak their original language.  The rest spoke nothing but teekchew Chinese.  I soon saw that it was no Dutch that they spoke, but a language more like Malay.  The papers too which they spoke of consisted of a series of Roman letters running one into another, without any order, and evidently copied down by some one who knew nothing of their use.  None of my visitors could read a single one of them.  Occasionally a Dutch word occurred, and a numeral here and there.  The Dutch consul at Amoy, Mr. De Grijs, tried to decipher them, but entirely failed. They brought me two coats for my inspection, one was a large white smock, plain, but bulky, with short sleeves, not unlike a surplice.  They said it was the dress of a priest.  It had probably belonged to a Dutch missionary.  The other consisted of two pieces of dark purple silk, embroidered with small gilt dragons, like Japanese stuff, with a piece of dingy cloth sewn between.  They called it an outer coat, but it looked to me more like the cover of a couch.  These people are generally distinguished by the Chinese as the Pepos.
In answer to my enquiries on the habits and customs of these people, Padre Sainz supplied me with the following information: that the men dress quite like the Chinese, but the women enwrap their loins with a cloth which reaches below the knee, like the women at Manila.  They only wear this, however, on certain occasions; that the men shave their heads and wear Chinese queues; that the women part their hair in the middle and twist it up behind in a semi-globular knot, but he had not paid particular attention to them; that these people are subject to the Chinese government, but elect their own elders; that he had visited several villages peopled by this race but could only recollect the name of four, two on the east of Taiwanfoo, Sin-kang and Kun-Hieng, and two near the mountains inland of Ape's hill, Ban-kin-shan, and Toa-kun-lieng, the nearest to Ape's hill being Ban-kin-Shan, and to Taiwanfoo, Sin-kang; and that considering how many of the words that they still retain of their own native language are similar to the dialects of the Philippines, and that that is the nearest Indian country to the island of Formosa, the priest goes [on] to say that he believes he is right in conjecturing that the Pepos are of Malay origin, as are also those of the Philippines.
In the mountains of the south we have another tribe of Aborigines who demand our sympathy far more than the last, as they have struggled hard and are still struggling against the ever encroaching Chinese settler, to maintain their territory and independence.  The plains have been entirely snatched from them as well as the lower-hill ranges, but where the mountains are sufficiently covered with forest and of sufficient height to enable them to repel the usurper they have succeeded in doing so.  To the north I am told their mountain possessions do not extend ##--SPLIT--## much beyond the latitude of Taiwanfoo, but southwards right to the cape end of the island, having the steep rocky coast on the west from Langkeaou southwards and the whole of the east that flanks them.  I had no opportunities of visiting this people, and am therefore almost entirely endebted to the kindness of Padre Sainz for the few notes that I possess of them.  "The borders of the mountain territory of this people is [sic] about ten leagues distant from Ape's hill, the Chinese possessions in their neighborhood being occupied by emigrants, chiefly from [p. 5] Swatow and a few from Changchow.  The men wear their hair long, and in some cases bound round the head with a kerchief.  (The women the Priest again unfortunately did not pay any attention to, and could not therefore inform me how they made their toilet.) These people barter their bark-fibre manufactured cloth, grass mats, deer horns, &c., for Chinese gods.  They possess buffaloes with which they till their hills, but have no other cattle.  Their houses are built like grottoes against the large rocks, being constructed of small stones plastered between logs of wood.  They have several villages which are governed by their own officers quite independent of the Chinese.  Three villages called Tanasia, Ka-chassan, and Kisien were said together to contain from six to seven thousand inhabitants, but no general estimate of the number of this people could be obtained.  They are doubtless of the Malay race and very similar to the natives of Luzon."
The priest further told me that the male Kalees he saw were nearly naked, with the exception of the two sons of their chief, who had on pretty coats.  The women wore cloths wrapt round their loins.  The arms they use are lances, knives, and bows and arrows, which they employ against their enemies as well as for the chase.  They drink no other spirits than that supplied them by the Chinese, namely – the rice spirit, known to Europeans as Samshoo.  They pour this into cups made of hollow bamboo, and before drinking dip the two first fingers of their right hand into the liquor and sprinkle it in three different directions.  After this ceremony they offer the cup to all present before they put it to their own lips.  The first time the priest attempted to reach the country of Kalees he passed through the Chinese territory, when the colonists, notwithstanding his disguise, discovered he was a foreigner and resisted his passage.  He was not, however, to be defeated, so he took a boat and passing down the coast to where the hills descend directly to the sea, after some difficulty effected a landing.  The Kalees came down armed, and asked him what he wanted.  He replied he had come on a purely friendly visit, whereupon they demanded a present for their chief.  He said he was a poor man, so his gifts were trifling, and then presented them with two empty beer bottles, with which they returned highly satisfied to their chief.  Shortly after they returned and two of them forming a sedan with their arms carried him in state up hill to their village.  He had an interview with the two sons of the chief, and fired off a revolver for their amusement.  They were much pleased with the firearm and proposed that he should come and live amongst them and aid them in driving the Chinamen from the country.  The priest was very well satisfied with his reception, and hoped that the Procurador at Macao would give him permission to settle among these wild mountaineers, for he had great hopes of making a promising propaganda among them.
My own acquaintance with this people is very limited.  When circumnavigating the island in 1857 [actually 1858], I had the pleasure of seeing a few women, who were married to Chinese at Pongle and Langkeaou.  They were much browner than ordinary Chinese, and had their hair plaited and entwined with red cotton after the manner of their people, but they were otherwise dressed like Chinawomen.  At Laileaou a Chinamen named Bancheang, of large landed property, traded with the Kalees of the hills, and was on very friendly terms with them.  He was constantly at variance with the Chinese authorities who had outlawed him, but could not touch him, as he ##--SPLIT--## was so well defended by his numerous Chinese dependents, and the large body of Aborigines at his beck [sic].  This man was wedded to a Kalee, but she was shy and would not show during our visit.  He evidently considered himself vastly superior to Europeans, for he evinced to us none of that formal civility characteristic of a Chinese gentlemen.  Europeans have hitherto had no transactions with the Kalees.  Ships, frequently, in bad weather find it convenient to put into a small harbour at the south cape, and when any attempt is made to land, the natives, not understanding their meaning, come down armed and offer resistance.
The Chinese seem only to acknowledge two races of independent aborigines, which they distinguish by the names Kalee hwan (Kalee foreigner) and Chin hwan (raw foreigner), the mountains of the south being held by the former, and those of the north, from about the latitude of Taiwanfoo, by the latter.
On moving the Consulate to Tamsuy River, on the N.W. coast, I made every exertion to visit the Chin hwan, The borders of their hill range was [sic] about 30 miles from my house, so I sent up a Chinese servant to find quarters for me within easy march of the hut of a Tongsoo or interpreter, whom some of the hill tribes were in the habit of [p. 6] visiting for the purposes of barter.  On the 19th of April, 1862, one day's hot walk of some 25 miles brought me to the apartments I had engaged near the banks of the river, the descent of which to our Consulate occupied a little more than half the day, though the ascent, owing to the many rapids, was too long and tedious for me to undertake.  On the following day we started, at an early hour, for the land of the Kweiyings.  Our directions were due east, where we could see the bush-clad range peering above the nearer cleared hills.  We marched across the dried river-course to a ferry, where we crossed the river.  On the opposite side the hill came perpendicularly down to the water's edge, and from the blueness of the water close to it seemed to glide down precipitously some depth beneath its surface.  A pretty glade of blossoming evergreens partially covered the near side, and extended its shade and shelter to some cozy huts hard by.  By these huts the road wound up the hill.  We ascended, walked along its flattened top through rows of tea-plants looking in fine condition, and again descended into a village, the last of the Chinese in this direction.  The inhabitants were chiefly agricultural, and some of them men of money.  We then turned towards the river, which wound away on the right, and keeping along its banks for a mile and a half reached the solitary long mud and stone-built hut, divided into two rooms, inhabited by the interpreter.  It was a sad hovel, with a filthy table in the first,  and a dirty old bed in the second room.  A wood fire was blazing in the front room, and filled it with smoke.  The interpreter, an unmannerly Chinese, took us into the inner room, which was partly crowded with Chinese, and sitting on the bed I found two savages.  The meeting was curious enough.  They stood and stared at us in astonishment, though with no sign of fear.  The interpreter told them that we were also foreigners like themselves, and had come to visit them.  They sat down again and examined us, and exchanged pipes of tobacco.  After expressing admiration at our guns, they wanted to rush out and see us fire them.  We took down many of their words, and after a chat went out with them.  The elder savage ran to a distance, seized a plank, and put it up for a target, setting a leaf in the centre for a bull's eye.  I fired at it, and strange enough, though the shot scattered all round, yet not one hit the bull's eye.  The savage smiled, picked up his matchlock, and took up his position at my distance from the mark.  His matchlock, a Chinese one, was loaded with ball.  He fired, as the Chinese do, from the elbow, and hit the target about nine inches from the centre.  The distance was about 40 yards; the board was about three inches thick, yet the bullet went right through.  I loaded my gun with a cartridge, and doubled the distance.  ##--SPLIT--## The shot covered the mark.  This astonished the savage somewhat.  But he was much more taken with my Sharp's rifle, at its breach-loading process; and when I gave the sight its full elevation, and fired along the river, the splash of the ball in the distant water drew a cry of astonishment from both savage and Chinamen.  They took a fancy for the rifle, and wanted to barter for it; the breach-loading pistol also took their fancy, but they found great difficultly in pulling the trigger.  I showed them my watch, which they regarded as a piece of magic, crying out, "Kis-kis." In addressing me they used the expression "Tyon," which might either be from the Malay Tuon (Sir), or the Chinese Tajin (Your Excellency).  Late in the day several women, with children in arms, and one fine-looking young man arrived, and soon after an elderly man made his appearance, with the perspiration trickling down his face and body, laden with the head, neck, and parts of the back of a fine buck, the velvety antlers having been cut away.  The old man was dark, with a wrinkled skin, and short hair.  He could speak a little Chinese.  The younger men and women were fairer than ordinary Chinese, with nut-brown complexions instead of the yellow hue.  They had much of the Malay cast of countenance, with well-formed eyes and noses.  All were tattoed on the forehead with three series of short lines, the skin being raised and blue.  The tattoing is done with needles and Indian ink.  The lines are in three compact square forms, one above the other, the upper and lower consisting of eight lines each, the middle one of six.  In the old man the tattoo had nearly worn away.  The youth is tattoed at the age of sixteen.  When he is well into his teens, and has qualified himself for a wife by the decapitation and abduction of the cranium of some enemy, he is tattoed under the lower lip with a square of eight lines.  One young man of sixteen, with long hair parted down the middle, and loosely secured behind with a string of white shell beads, which also wound his head in a circlet, was not marked on the chin.  I asked if he were a woman.  At this he was highly indignant, and to show his indignation kept thumping the back of his head with the palm of his hand.  When a woman is married she has her face tattoed [p. 7] from ear to ear; first with three simple lines; below these a series of X between two other lines; then two more lines and another row of X; and below all four more simple lines, The interpreter was married to a savage woman, who was habited in the ordinary dress of a Chinese female, and certainly looked like a very ugly Amoy woman, except that she was adorned on the forehead with the usual tattoo mark.  Her resemblance to a Chinese girl elicited a remark from my guide worth noting.  He said she looked so like a woman, he could scarcely believe she was a foreigner.  The offspring of this interesting couple, a boy and girl, were Chinese even to the eyes.  The male savages squatted about the table, smoking and chatting, while the females arranged themselves in a row along the bottom of the wall attending to their little ones, which were nearly all carried suspended by a dirty cloth in front.  Both men and women were squalid in the extreme, the legs and arms of the men being daubed with deer's blood, to make them proof against scratches from the briars.  As the day grew hot they cast aside their plaids, quite exposing their lower limbs, with the most perfect nonchalance, in the presence of the women.  They all wore necklaces of white flattened square-cut bits of shell, run through with a string.  Some had in addition blue, red, and yellow bead necklaces, with different gear, such as tobacco-pouches, gun-primers, &c., attached to them.  All had the drop of of [sic] the ear bored with one large hole, in which often was stuck a short piece of wood half an inch thick.  One pretty girl had instead a worked ornamental stick with a red tuft in front.  They also wore triangular white flat shell pendants attached to the ear by a small string of blue, red, and white beads.  Some had tufts of dirty yellow stuff attached to their necklaces, which appeared to be charms.  A well-made bag hung under the arm against the side, suspended by a variegated cloth strap from the opposite shoulder.  The only arms they carried were knives and matchlocks, which they procure by barter from the Chinese. ##--SPLIT--##  After smoking, the males went and squatted by the females, and bowls of a sweet peculiarly-flavoured Chinese liquor, made of rice and potatoes, called Wookiape, diluted with water, went round.  I squatted among them to get a better view of them, when one savage, putting his arm round my neck, dragged my face to touch his, and holding the bowl up, endeavoured to pour the liquor into his own and my mouth at the same time.  Finding I objected, he turned to his next neighbour and made the loving potation.  This he repeated with all the males round, each time he drank, till the effects of the liquor begun to evince themselves on him, and he commenced talking in a loud and boisterous manner.  The savages were anxious to know if the Chinese were hiring us with our good guns to exterminate them.  They were were [sic] very anxious to procure any of our guns, but one of the women recommended them to only ask for the rifle.  They wanted to know whence we came, we replied from China; they said they would like to go to China, at which the Chinese laughed loud.  Most of the Chinese about there could speak some words of their language, and some were on very good terms with them, living on their hills, and associating with them.
The savages dress their dead in the plaid or wrapper, and bury them in the ground without raising any monuments to them or burn incense, but they plant a few trees on the spot.  The fact of their having no burial ceremonies convinces the Chinese of their savageness, as much as that of their wearing no trousers.
The interpreter's wife engaged to lead us across the river to the other side to show us some fine camphor trees, but as they feared the drunken savage who was getting very familiar would go with us, they hurried off.  The savage had patted me on the head, and wanted to hug me to swear eternal friendship, saying that we were of the right stamp with themselves, and ought to be allied in driving the shaven-headed rascals out of the country.  I wanted much to see their headey [sic] or chief, but they said it would take four days to bring him out, and that I should have to expend 15 dollars in making a feast for him.  The money I did not grudge, but I could not spare the time.
The savage clan appears to muster a very few for the size of their country.  They are said to have only seven sear or villages, containing each from 300 to 400 souls under four supreme and several petty chiefs.  Owing to the introduction of spirituous liquors and other unknown causes they seem to decrease in numbers, and will, probably before the lapse of many centuries entirely dwindle away before the steady advance of the rapidly increasing Chinese colonists.  The Chinese state with confidence that another century will witness their entire extermination, but this is perhaps too short a time for its fulfillment.  The nearest chief lived in a village called Gee-hing-sear said to be about two days journey from where we were.  He gloried in the name of Pai ho pai yet.  The land beyond his was controlled by the chief Yew-bin-ah-tan, who lived in the village of Tungsear.
[P. 8] These savages cultivate dry rice, sweet potatoes, and tobacco, but the greater part of what they consume is procured by barter from the Chinese.  They use the hand in eating, not being cleanly enough even to wash the blood and filth off before they begin.  The mark on the forehead of all is called Lehoey.  That on the chin is a square of eight lines and is called Kabai.  Some of the women wear, as an apology for drawers, short pieces of cloth from the knee downwards, secured with pieces of coarse string tied round above and below.  The married women wore turbans of blue cloth into which they stuck their pipes, while the maidens carried ##--SPLIT--## theirs in their hair.  Both sexes part their hair down the middle for a short distance, and mostly adorn it with a circlet of coloured beads, which was twisted round into the knot behind.  The prettiest Miss took a fancy to my red silk sash, so I took it off and made her a present of it.  I tied it round her waist.  She looked modestly, and blushed at what she evidently considered a liberty, especially as her clansmen laughed and made remarks.  She walked about for some time with it on among the admiring gaze of the savages, and then took it down to the river, and washed it, and hung it up to dry.  As a return present she brought me a couple of variegated bands of her own manufacture and desired me to choose one.  The Chinese laughed at my simplicity in exchanging my sash, worth 5 dollars, for one worth only a few hundred cash, and wherever I afterwards went the story was carried forward.
The mode of greeting among these savages is to walk up to the stranger smiling with the right palm extended, and to thrust it with force against his stomach, and then with the left hand to pat him on the back.  If he is friendly disposed he smiles in return, and repays you the compliment in the same manner.
The river winds away among the hills with numerous rapids at intervals, and every mile or so between deep show-flowing water with ferry boats sculled by Chinese.  At the spot we had reached, the river about 40 yards wide, divided the wood-covered hills of the savage from the cleared hills of the men.  But on the opposite side close to the foot of the wooded hills occurred a few acres of flat land, which some enterprising Chinese had planted with rice, and built himself a cabin on.  The first range of hills is considered common land, and any trespass on them, except by a favoured few who are allowed to sojourn there in huts, is repelled by the matchlock of the lurking savage.  In the pursuit of game or murder the savage is much guided by certain small species of birds, the notes of which are either taken as encouragement or warning.  If thought to be propitious they follow the flight of the bird, and where it drops there they hide and watch for sport.  But the deer hunting which is at this season of spring prosecuted with vigour for the sake of the velvet-covered horns which are bartered to the Chinese, is practiced by beating in circles and closing in on the driven animals.  The greater number of the deer killed are the unspotted species (Cervus swinhoii) which frequent a lower range to the spotted species (C. taiwanus).  The flesh of goats is considered strong and tough, and these animals are therefore not much sought after.  The Chinese give a preference to the muntjae (Cervulus Reevesii) but they have no idea of letting their meat hang to give it a gamy flavour.  Attention to the notes of birds prevails to a certain extent among the Chinese, and they objected to my shooting the crows, because they always set up a laou-wa note of lamentation when the savages killed any of the men.
I procured the ordinary dress of the male and female savages, as also their state costumes, all of which I brought home with me.  The dresses of the chiefs are more shewy than those of the people.  They are woven by hand by the people themselves of hemp, plaintain fibre, and the fibre of some unknown trees.  The red and blue threads introduced are unravelled from woollens of European manufacture, procured through the Chinese.  The gaudier dresses are worn on holidays and state occasions, as also on the celebration of marriages, at which last ceremony the whole village assembles round the door of the brides' [sic] father, and the skull won in combat, which entitles the bridegroom to the privilege of the married state is brought forward on the occasion and presented to the bride, who prepares a draught in it, by mixing spirits with the brains, and the offers it as a lovely cup to all present, commencing with the chief and ending with the bridegroom.  After this the bride remains in her father's house, and does not live with her ##--SPLIT--## husband till she has attained the age of 36.  Before that period, though regarded as a wife, she is not considered to hold the position of a matron.  This account I gathered from the Chinese on the spot, and as it tallies with the Dutch records of the customs of other Formosian tribes, I suppose there must  be some truth in it.
[P. 9] We returned to our lodgings at the farm at Chin-tam, and in the evening were beset by our hosts' country cousins.  Most of them could speak some words of the savage lingo, and kept plaguing me with them, wondering why I did not understand.  They said that surely all foreigners were of the same origin, and ought naturally to speak the same language, evidently setting down the world in their amusing self-conceit as peopled by foreigners, and the men par excellence, the latter having of course the lion's share.  After making several attempts to impart to them some small information about the countries of Europe, and observing their mild smile of unbelief, I thought to myself "Where ignorance is bliss, &c." and let them indulge in their harmless jabber.  Nevertheless in all business transactions the Chinese readily enough admits that the Kapan (ship) foreigner is a powerful customer, and as many remarked "the hill foreigners disgusting brutes are your notsai (slaves)," either referring to the former occupation of the Dutch, or as a term of comparative reproach.  Generally speaking, however, the savages are termed "the foreigners, " and we are distinguished as "the red-haired foreigners." The Mandarins and literati throughout Formosa are too apt to distinguish us as the Ejin  or Barbarian men.  This also prevails throughout China, and so objectionable was the term considered that Lord Elgin introduced a special clause into the last treaty forbidding the use of the term in official documents; the word Fan-jin (foreign man) or Wai-kwo-jin (outside country man) being required to be substituted in its stead.  The latter is so long and unwieldy as never to become of general use, while the former, especially in Formosa where it is also applied to the savage, is particularly odious.  The truth is, the Amoy dialect, and indeed the Chinese language, is badly provided with terms for outsiders, or people who do not wear tails and acknowledge Mantchoo rule, and having but the one word Fan they apply it to all and everything foreign, hence its application also to the savage, of whom when speaking discriminatingly they sometimes prefix the word raw.  By being applied to the savage, the word in Formosa has got a shade worse meaning than it has at Amoy.  In writing, therefore, to use a shorter term than outside countryman, they distinguish the European from the too fan  or (foreigner of the soil) by the objectionable word E (barbarian) suffixing jin (or man) in order to modify its harshness.
A story was told me by one of my Chinese followers of how, some years ago, the family of a relation of his was destroyed by the savages, all except a little girl, who was carried off by them.  This girl, now grown up, he has since seen.  She has become a complete savage, and hates the Chinese as much as any of them.  Chinamen to escape justice not unfrequently run among the savages and, adopting their costume, become of them.  Boys and even girls, preferring a life in the woods have been known to run away from their parents and join the savages.
The huts of these Kweiyings are made of bark and rough planks, held together with rattans, and thatched with palm leaves.  The women and old men do much of the heavy work and carry burdens, while the young men engage in the chase.  Cultivation of fields is also chiefly carried on by the women. ##--SPLIT--##
The Chinese barter rice, sugar, opium refuse, European red cloth, matchlocks, ammunition, beads, &c., with the Chinhwan for deer's horns, hides, venison, fibre cloth, hemp thread, mats, baskets, &c.
In 1857 [1858], when circumnavigating Formosa in H.M.S. "Inflexible" we had the pleasure of seeing some of a savage race on the eastern coast, allied to the Kweiyings.  They were very similar in appearance, but bore a different name, and as I has no opportunities of having an interview with them, I cannot say whether their dialect was identical.  I will extract a short note about them from a narrative of the voyage I read before the North China branch of the Asiatic Society at Shanghai.
"On the 17th of June we were off a place in lat. 24 6' 18", where a river is marked on the chart.  A ravine runs between the hills, but there is only a small mountain stream.  The ship was about eight hundred yards from land, yet we got no sounding at one hundred and fifteen fathoms.  The morning was fine with very little sea, so we pulled away for shore in the gig, steering towards a collection of huts at the foot of the hills.
"When only one hundred and fifty yards from the shore, we sounded with eleven fathoms of boat line, and got no bottom; when at about fifty yards, we had eight and a half fathoms.  Several natives appeared on the beach, many of whom were Chinese; but among them we could distinguish six men who were almost in a state of nudity, wearing only a piece of cloth round the waist with a flap in front.
[P. 10] "These latter carried spears in their hands, and wore sabers in sheaths stuck through their girdles and hanging behind.  Their hair was short and fringed on the forehead; behind it hung loose.  Their arrows, singularly enough, had no feathered shaft.  The surf was too great for the gig to go in close to the shore, so we called the Chinese, who were pushing a boat into the water, to come out to us.  They were just going to shove off when four of these savages jumped into their boat, and the Chinese, finding they could not hold them back, beckoned us to go away.  The savages meanwhile, enraged that they could not get out at us, began to shake their spears and brandish their sabers with threatening gestures; but a shot fired over their heads soon put them to flight and they took shelter behind a mound.  The Chinese then came off to us; and taking one of them into out boat, we asked him various questions.  These savages, according to his account, were called Ty-lo-kok, and their tribes [sic] numbered about four thousand; they inhabited the surrounding woody hills and subsisted on sweet potatoes, taro, and deer's flesh.  The bare patches of land we saw on the hills were cleared away by them for cultivation.  The greater part of the hills was thickly covered with camphor trees, and the boat they were in was made of camphor wood.  He said there were about two hundred Chinese in the valley, who subsisted on the produce of their fishing.  They had been sent there, many years ago, by the Mandarins (and were probably convicts); if we were to kill any of the savages they would revenge themselves on the Chinese, for the savages had arms, the Chinese had none.  A village that used to exist a little further up the coast, where we could see a wreath of blue smoke rising, had been burnt by the savages and every one killed.  One man who had lived here fifteen years, had never seen or heard of any wreck.  Foreign ships had before been seen passing by that place but none had ever come so near to the land as ours." ##--SPLIT--##
The only other tribe of whom I have any facts to relate are the Shuh-fan, or in the local dialect, Siek-hwan (cooked or ripe foreigners), of Sawo harbour, and the Komalan or Kapsuylan plain.  On the voyage referred to we entered Sawo harbour, which appears to be the boundary of the Chinese territory on the east side.  Here in a little bay on the left we found a small village of the Siek-hwan.  Some of the males of this people had loose hair, but not a few of the younger of them had their heads shaved in the Chinese fashion.  They were a shade or so darker than the Chinese, with a Malayan cast of countenance.  Of the women some were brown, others nearly fair; while many had European physiognomies.  A few wore coats, or something thrown over their shoulders; but the majority had no other covering than a wrapper round the loins, secured with a cloth girdle.  Their hair hung loose, with a white or red fillet laid just above the forehead.  Most of these people smoked pipes, or rolls of tobacco shaped like cigars.
One of the men spoke a little Chinese, and we got him to interpret to the others.  When we inquired about their origin, they said they only knew that they belonged to the soil.  They could not even tell us their own ages, having apparently no means of noting them.  They would not be called Chin-hwan or "raw foreigners"; they were Hwanah, or foreigners same as we were.  They seemed to be as much afraid of the mountain savages as were the Chinese themselves.
These people, both in the construction of their houses, and in their mode of living, resemble the Chinese much more than do the blood-thirsty race we had seen a few days previously.  A quieter and more inoffensive class of people could hardly be found anywhere.  They had never before seen a foreign steamer, and in the afternoon came off in crowds, and spent the remainder of the day in going round and round the ship, both men and women, sculling themselves in their point-ended proas or canoes, chanting a most peculiar air.
A little above Sawo, up the river Polosinnawan (marked Kalewan river in the chart) we found several villages of Komalans on its banks.  They were exceedingly civil and good-natured, and showed us about their tree-ensconced residences.  Their houses, built off the ground on posts, were constructed chiefly of wooden logs with thatched roofs, and had boarded floors.  The people were governed by head men of their own race, who were responsible to the Chinese commandant of the chief Chinese village, Le-tuk-kan, further up the river.  In these villages the dark people appeared to be in better circumstances than at Sawo, but further up we met with crowds of Komalans in a very squalid state, wandering about from place to place, and depending on charity for support.  The Chinese deprive them of their land for any trivial fault, and drive them away in herds in the most heartless manner.  The poor creatures are greatly in the minority in these [p. 11] plains, and it will not take many years before their small bands will entirely disappear before the fast increasing usurpers.  The women here dress their hair neatly, with three or four folds of red thread wrapped round, the whole surmounted by a woven garland of of [sic] green creeping plants.  Their ears were bored with several holes, and five or six thin white-metal rings, two inches or so in diameter, were thrust through each hole.  These rather loaded the ears, but the effect was very far from unbecoming.  Over the doorways of their houses they had the heads of deer, wild pigs, and other wild animals nailed, and the walls inside were hung with bows and arrows (the shafts of the arrows having no feathers).  These arms, apparently momentos of their better days of independence, the families were very loath to part with.
The aborigines of the Polosinnawan river and Sawo call themselves Komalan or Kapalan, whence the Chinese name of the district on the east side, which once belonged to them.  In the Government map they are spoken of as the Aleshe foreigners.  In the ##--SPLIT--## small vocabulary I possess of their language I can trace no similarity in their dialect (except in such general words as those for silver and tobacco) with that of their neighbours, the Kweiying mountaineers; whereas their numerals are identical with those of the Kalees of the South, from whom they are now separated by a long range of high mountains.  Their words for horse and buffalo are evidently of Spanish origin, and this they doubtless derived from the Spanish settlers at Kelung.  It is perhaps not improbable that they are the descendants of some Kalees that the Spaniards might have introduced from the South on their occupation.  This, however, is mere speculation, though it is hard otherwise to account for two peoples possessing the same numerals unchanged for so many generations, existing at such distances apart, with the vast territory between hostile and impassable, and populated by other tribes of distinct dialects.
I have thus been able to gather a few notes on five tribes of the aboriginal races of Formosa – the Kalees of the southern mountains, the Kweiyings of the northern mountains, the Tylokoks of the N.E. mountains, the Komalans of the N.E. plains, and the Pepos of Sinkang.  The last are almost entirely amalgamated with the Chinese, and the last but one are fast disappearing.  Of a sixth tribe, the people of Sakkam, who have entirely disappeared, we know the most through Dutch writings, as they were the natives with whom the Hollanders first came in contact on their island of Tayowan, for Sakkam was in those days a log-hut town on the site of the present city of Taiwanfoo.  It was within the clanship of the larger town of Teƒurang, which was "in a valley near the highlands, where Fort Zelandia stands." "The inhabitants of this town," says Ogilby, quoting Georgius Candidius, a Dutch minister of the gospel, who resided in Formosa, A.D., 1628 (Atlas Chinensis, vol. ii., p. 8) "were rude and savage, robust, and almost of a gigantic size, not black like the Caffers, and counted it no immodesty to go stark naked.  Their women well built for stowage, short, and inclining to grossness, wearing apparel; yet twice a day they strip themselves, and are not ashamed to bathe and wash in public.  These, though a barbarous people, were kind to the Netherlanders, heartily entertaining them with their own, though mean, yet wholesome fare." Of the language spoken by this people I have a vocabulary translated into English, and published at Batavia by the late Dr. Medhurst, from the Dutch of Gilbertus Happart, 1650.  The author of the original work was one of the Dutch missionaries to Formosa, and having been directed to prepare a dictionary of the Sakkam or Favorlang[1] dialect of the Formosan language, this work was the result.  The Woord Bock gives eleven districts under the government of the Dutch, but as the translator had to take the words from a most illegibly written M.S., I prefer following the orthography of the Atlas Chinensis.  Nothing is said there of the extent of the dominions of the Dutch, who, from accounts, I should imagine had never a very strong hold on Formosa.  "Of the many villages," writes Ogilby, "the prime, and of chiefest note, are Sinkan, Mandau, Soolang, Bakeloang, Taƒƒacan, Tiƒulucan, Teopan, and Teƒurang." Mr. Crawford has kindly looked over this vocabulary, and marked the words that are at all similar to the Malay language.  Of such words there appear to be only twenty-eight, including four numerals.  These words from their character one might easily imagine have been of recent introduction, and not improbably from the Malay servants of the Dutch.  Compared with the other Formosan dialects, the Teƒurang again differs as much from them all as they do from [p. 12] one another; the chief traceable connection being found in some of the numerals.  It bears, on ##--SPLIT--## the other hand, a slight approach to the Japanese, in the intervention of no between two substantives to form a genitive.  It is not, however, improbable that this was borrowed from the Japanese during their settlement at Tayowan  before the Dutch arrived.
I was at first inclined to suppose that the fairer Kweiying mountaineers were of a different stock to the dark Kalees and other inhabitants of the lowlands, but I have since observed the colour varies considerably in individuals, both among the Kweiyings and the Komalans.  The strange diversity of dialects among cognate tribes existed to a more exaggerated extent in Formosa than perhaps in any wild country, but in no country, perhaps, except North America, have the inhabitants been so split up into small warring clans.  "They are governed," remarks Ogilby, "by several lords, not depending on nor acknowledging any superior; so that each town being a republic, they still have wars, and are at difference one with another; town against town, village against village, insomuch that peace never set foot in that isle."
The notes of "David Wright, a Scotsman," who resided in Formosa during the occupation of the Dutch, are given in the Atlas Chinensis.  They are interesting, as descriptive of the appearance and manners of the tribes of the plain, many of whom have long ceased to exist.  I shall therefore make no excuse in here giving some short extracts from them.
Description of the Natives. – The men of Formosa are very large and strong-limbed, especially those of the plain country; for those of the mountains are generally of lesser stature, and the women yet smaller than either.  The men of the plain are full-faced, and without beards, not naturally but by continual plucking out of the hair so soon as it buds.  They have great eyes, flat noses, large breasts, and very long ears, which they count a great ornament, and have bored through and tentered out broad with a horn; some hang a round tablet in the hole, painted and carved after their manner; others coloured shells; but chiefly on festival days, and when they are to appear before their idols, for on other days they leave their long extended lugs, which cover their collar-bone, naked and without ornament, hanging almost half-way down to their naked breasts.  Their hair, as black as jet, is also very long, and by most worn as they do in Europe.  They are of a tawny or olivaster complexion, not much fairer than the mulatto.  The women of Midag are bright, like the golden yellow, as likewise those of Sootan Nouwe, and the island Lammay.
Their Dress and Manners. – Their habit, or summer-garment, is only a thin cotton coat, made wide, tied fast on the breast by two corners, and then put under one of their arms; so that generally one side of their bodies is covered, and the other bare; about the middle close-girt, and hanging down below their knees.  They neither wear shoes nor stockings, but sometimes a kind of pumps or sandals, made of goat-skins, which they fasten on the top of their feet.
"In the winter time they wear tiger's, leopard's, bear's and other wild beasts' skins.  The people of Soulang went habited like the Dutch, being still conversant among them.
"Before the Spaniards and Hollanders came thither, the inhabitants went naked; which savage custom those that live in the mountains still continue, wearing only a small cloth before.
"The women's habit agrees almost with the men's, only with this difference, that they tie clouts about their legs, like spatterdashes, and have short coats, like a half-shirt without sleeves, ##--SPLIT--## which reach down to their middle, and under that a cotton cloth falling down to their knees.  Their heads are filleted with a piece of silk, about a yard and a half long, whose two ends stick out like horns on their foreheads; and none of them even wear shoes.  But every woman hath commonly a great pig running after her, as we use to have a dog.
"The men paint the skin of their breasts, backs, and arms with a colour which remains in the flesh, and will never be got out, and by them accounted a great bravery.  About their necks and arms they hang glass beads strung, and iron armlets, which sit very straight like an elbow gauntlet, so narrow is the wrist, that it seems impossible almost how they could be passed over the hand; and on their legs they have anklets of white shells joined together like a scallop [sic] lace.
"The men of Tokdadekol wear as an ornament a long cane, which they stick behind their backs at a girdle, the other end hanging over their heads, having a white or red flag about two handfuls broad.
[P. 13] "Their heads on holiday's [sic] are gay with cock's feathers, and their arms and legs with bear's tails.  The skins of deer spread on the ground serve them for beds and blankets.
"They never venture out to sea, but fish in rivers with small canoes.  They are dexterous in the use of the bow, are expert swimmers, and excellent runners.
"The inhabitants smoke tobacco, though it grows not there, but is brought to them from China.  Their pipes are thin reeds of cane with stone beads."
War. – "When marching out to a fight, they take the skulls trophies of former victories which they carefully preserve, and stopping the mouth of the skull, full of rice, they invoke after this manner – ‘You relics, though of our enemies  O let your spirits departed from you march with us into the field, and help us to obtain the victory; which if you please to grant, and we have the day, we promise to present you with continued offerings, and reckon you among the number of our favouring gods.' But if they be routed and some slain, whose bodies they could not bring off, they return to their towns with great lamentations, then feed their fancies with representations, dressed up as babies in clouts, like those that were lost in the field, whom they inter, as if the very same persons, and get the priestess to make offerings to the deceased souls, conjuring them not to go amongst, nor by intelligence help their adversaries.
"War is often decided by a struggle between a champion from either side; the side that owns the victorious champion being allowed the victory.  The conqueror returns with the head of his departed antagonist on his lance, and about him his tribe flock, dancing and singing and making merry.
"Before they march into the field they superstitiously observe the dreams which they had the night preceding; and augur from the singing and flying of a certain small bird called Aidak.  If this bird meets them flying with a worm in his bill, they take it for an infallible sign, that they shall conquer their enemies.  But if the bird flies from them, or pass by them, they are so much disheartened by the ill-omen, that they return home, and will not engage until they have better signs.  They always choose one most approved for valour as their general, whom they style ##--SPLIT--## Tamatuwa, who never takes the field, nor engages in battle, before he has by offerings endeavoured to pacify and implore the gods for good success.
"They shew no mercy, nor give any quarter, but slaughter all, not sparing women or children.  Neither are they satisfied with killing them, but carry home the heads of the slain as trophies of their victory, being received at their return by their wives and children with great rejoicing expressed by singing and dancing.  They enter the towns with the heads of their enemies erected upon long poles, which they present to their gods seven succeeding nights.  After having taken off all the flesh, they hang up in their houses the skulls as ornaments, as we in Europe use paintings or statues."
The Chase. – "Their hunting, which lasts never less than twelve days, is performed sometimes by few, and at other times with many people, who for the most part use snares and canes, and also spears, and bows and arrows.  When they have appointed a great match, they build a house in the fields, wherein they hang all their implements.  Before they go out they tell to one another the dreams they had the preceding night, and also neglect not augurial observations; insomuch that if the bird Aydak [sic] meet them, they count it a good omen.  But if it flies either on the right or left side of them they put off the sport till some other time.
"Before commencing they offer sacrifice to the two gods that preside over hunting; and from the first quarry of every sort of wild creature they take a snip from the tail, mouth, heart, and kidneys, which they present in form to these deities.
"Coming back from their recreation, they return thanks to the gods in the house which they built to put their arms and hunting necessaries in; praying that those which come after them may have no success, nor kill anything.  Lastly they pull down the house, having first sent for the women to carry home the taken venison, who coming thither, bring abundance of cheering tope to make merry with.
"They often kill at one of these great hunting matches 800, 1000, nay sometimes 2000 head of deer.  The venison is salted and dried, and put away for future use."
I will add a few extracts from the notes of "Georgius Candidius," also contained in the work above quoted, as further illustrating the manners, customs, and ceremonies [p. 14] of the Formosan Aborigines of the once Dutch possessions, many of which would apply equally as well to the wild races I have met and heard of.  Indeed, notwithstanding the dissimilarity of language, a great similarity appears to have existed and to exist between the domestic habits of the various tribes.  I have, in all these extracts, been obliged to abbreviate and to somewhat modernize the long quaint prosy style of the original.
Clan Warfare. – "First one town begins sending the other a bold defiance; this is followed by twenty in a party, which row in their boats to their enemy's village, near which they lie in ambuscade till night.  At night they land and explore the fields culling out private huts where the graver sort, the married people, dwell at their ease and pleasure.  These they suddenly surprise and lop off heads, hands, and feet, which they carry home as trophies of victory.  In these attacks they spare none, man, woman, or child.  Often, however, the town they are about to attack takes alarm in time, and gives them a hot reception.  Their defensive arms are shields so ##--SPLIT--## large that they cover their whole bodies; their offensive, darts and falchions.  Each warrior that has won the head of an enemy, carries the trophy home on the point of his spear, and is everywhere joyfully received and entertained.
"Every sixteen houses have their own temple to which they carry their bloody trophies.  The skull is boiled and cleaned and then strong spirits are poured upon the scalp.  The rejoicing after a victory continues for fourteen days.  These skulls are greatly prized by their possessors."
Domestic Government. – "Every petty village is in itself a small republick, governed by 12 magistrates who are chosen every second year.  The Chief qualification for the appointment being the attainment to the age of 50.  At expiry of office the hair is plucked off from their temples and each side of their heads as a mark of their having been in power.  The magistrates have little power, for all questions of importance are settled by general debate, in which all can claim an hearing; and the closing opinions of the appointed authorities is accepted or not as the people choose, They have, however, power to enforce the observance of religious rites and duties.  Youth is a slave to age, the old men exacting great respect from their juniors."
Marriage. – "The males must not marry till their 21st year, nor wear long hair till the 17th year.  Marriage is not attended with any ceremony except that of sending a present from the bridegroom to the wife's parents.  But after the first day the husband is not permitted to live under the same roof as his wife.  She works and supports herself in her father's house, and he attends to his daily avocations.  At night he has the privilege of visiting her room clandestinely in the dark, and is obliged to hurry away again before day dawn.  If they chance to meet by day, they pass each other like strangers, the husband not daring to speak to his wife without her license.  The charge of bringing up children is left to the care of the wife till they are above 21, when their father receives and disposes of them as he thinks fitting.  Wives, however, are not permitted to become mothers until they attain the age of 37, and a case is related where a young woman who had married early was obliged to have sixteen children destroyed within her before her age of discretion was attained.  The husband in his 40th year leaves his solitary abode and lives the rest of his days with his wife.  But upon the least quarrel they part; so that sometimes they change their old for new wives as often as once a month.  If the husband can shew just reason for the separation, he can recover the dowry he gave her, but otherwise she retains it.
"The youths and bachelors live in the temple, as in a college, where they keep the bachelor's place, though married, till such times as they go to live with their wives."
Dwellings. – "The Formosan houses are he [sic] handsomest and exactest built of any after the Indian manner.  They raise a foundation 6 feet high of firm clay; the walls consist of reeds and rushes, artificially strengthened with interweavings, having four doors opening to the four winds, but the prime buildings have eight.  The ornaments without and furniture within, are stags crested heads and other heads, arms, &c.  But they esteem most the skulls and bones of enemies killed by their own hands."
Funeral Obsequies. – "The corpse being laid out, after 24 hours, they elevate it upon a scaffold matted with reeds and rushes 4 feet high, near which they make a fire, that so the corpse may by degrees dry.  The friends of the deceased flock together to the spot, with large supplies of flesh and spirits.  A drum, made out of a hollow tree, is beaten to give notice of the death, and ##--SPLIT--## large numbers of people assemble bringing [p. 15] more liquor with them, with which they ply the relations of the deceased, while the mourning dance is kept up outside the door in the following manner.  They take a trough like a chest, and turning the bottom upwards, the women get up two at a time, and standing back to back move their legs and arms in a dancing time and manner; which pace, or taboring tread, sends a kind of murmuring doleful sound from the hollow tree.  When these mounted couples are weary they come down and others supply their places.  This wake continues nine days, during which time the body lies parching by the fire, sending forth a noisome stench.  The corpse is then bathed nine times, wrapped up closely in a mat, and left on a raised platform protected from the weather for three years, when nothing remains but the bones.  These are then interred in the man's own house, with all the ceremony of feasts, as if he had but lately departed."
Treatment of the Sick. – "The ordering of their sick is no less unnatural and preposterous, for they use them worse than if the devil were their doctor; for instead of potion or pill, and the like, they have but one medicine for all diseases, and that is a dry halter, especially in the village Teopan.  For as soon as any person falls sick, and begins to complain, they prepare a tough cord instead of cordial, and putting a noose about his neck they hoist him up to the top of a house with a pulley kept for that purpose.  The rope is then suddenly slipped, and the patient comes down with a jolt, terminating in most cases fatally; but some do certainly mend upon this choking medicine."
Religion. – "They have no literature, and faithful memories are their only registers and records.  Certain young men are appointed to be instructed in the traditions from the elders.  They believe in future punishment for misdeeds of the flesh.  But their misdeeds or crimes are such as the following: not to go naked on appointed days; to wear clothes of silk; women to bear children before their 37th year; to fetch and eat oysters not in due season; and to venture to undertake any business whatsoever before they have observed good auguries from the notes of birds.  All these are unpardonable offences.  But murder, theft, lying, and forswearing have their qualifications, and are in general looked upon no more than peccadilloes and venial offences.
"An oath is ratified by breaking a straw.
"They believe in the immortality of the soul, but utterly deny the resurrection of the body.
"They worship several gods.  The first in rank are the good spirit (Tamagisanhach), and his wife (Tanankpada Agodalis), who control the destinies of mankind, and parcel out the blessings.  The supreme ruler is, however, supposed by them to be henpecked by his wife, for she is always thundering at him in loud peals over the mountain tops for not keeping to his good offices.
"The second is the evil spirit (Sariasing), whose duty in the universe is to disfigure and evilly-entreat man and his belongings, and who requires frequent and special propitiation.
"Then come Talaƒula and Tapaliapo, their gods of war, to whom they appeal in times of war and bloodshed.
"The whole functions of divine service is [sic] performed by priestesses, called Inibs.  These pray and preach, and sacrifice to the gods.  Their chiefest offerings are stags' and boars' ##--SPLIT--## heads, presented in a charger, upon boiled rice and betel, with libations of strong drink.  This performed, two of the female order stand up before the congregation, and set forth the praise and honour of their gods, going on with that vehemency of expressions and loud declamations, that at last their hair stands upright, and their eyes, rolling, seem ready to start out of their heads; then, near the conclusion of these dire exhortations, they fall down in a trance, and oft lie so for an whole hour, whilst the people, gathering round, tear their throats, and cleave the air with hideous shrieks and cries.  When coming to themselves, in a cold and faint sweat, their limbs trembling, their teeth chattering, strangely discomposed, they tell the people that in their trance the gods appeared to them, unfolding mysterious things for their good, not yet to be expressed.  At the same time two other priestesses, mounting the battlements of the temple, stand at the gable ends, making afresh loud and long supplications to their gods, shaking and clapping their hands as in frantic distraction.  All the women, following their impudent example, throw off with their weeds all shame at once, and so tope and debauch, till they disgorge their two [sic] plentiful excess."
They appear to have had several public feasts, such as the Harvest Home, the Venus Fetes, and others, attended with much debauchery.  These are described at length by Ogilby.
[P. 16] "Besides this public worship in the temple, they offer in the open streets, and exercise private and domestic devotions, performing family duties daily in their own houses, to which those that please may repair, and join with them in their idol service.
"These Inibs they also consult concerning fair or foul weather, or of future events, which they seem to foretell, and if bad, advise how to prevent.  The priestesses profess power over evil spirits, the devil, and all such deceitful tempters.  They perform the exorcism in a rant, with loud hectoring acclamations, drawing a falchion, with which they fence, strike, and thrust, flourishing it in the air, and making believe that thus they fight the fiend, and slice Satan into snippets.
"Yet these so savage people, blind with an overgrown ignorance, have lately (since the Hollanders' East India Company settled here, and built a fort for their defence and  conveniency of trade) without any very great difficulty embraced the doctrine of the Christian faith; which was the easier introduced, the inhabitants being their own governors, and under no supreme authority.  The whole conduct of their divine laws being left to a few frantic and ignorant women, who knew nothing but by tradition, made it the more easy to reduce them from their paganism; and finding little in their frenzied Inibs that might persuade them to persist in their former superstitions, many of them, with small difficulty, became good Christians."

For the two first of these I am indebted to Padre Sainz, and for the third to Captain Roper, of the schooner "Vindex." The fourth I took down myself from the mouths of the natives; and the few words in the fifth column are gathered from the printed vocabulary above referred to, being the only numerals therein given.  I have small vocabularies of each of these five dialects but not sufficient corresponding words to form a fair comparative list.  The ordinary words of the respective dialects that I have been able to compare bear a similar relation to one another, as do their numerals.  The numerals of the Kalees and Komalans, as I have before observed, are almost identical.  Judging from the numerals I do not think these languages differ more from one another than do the numerous dialects of Chinese.  That they have a relation to the Malay some of the numerals, and many other words prove, and it is not improbable that that relation would be found stronger if we could compare them with the languages of the intermediate tribes of the Philippine islands.
